structured_choices.data_source: Query logs, product catalog metadata, and click-through/add-to-cart events from the production e-commerce platform spanning 6 months (months 1–6). Manually judged relevance labels obtained via crowdsourced annotation on a stratified sample of 5,000 query-product pairs, drawn proportionally from head, mid-tail, and long-tail query frequency buckets, ensuring at least 500 pairs from synonym-heavy or ambiguous queries. Labels use 5-point relevance scale (irrelevant, poor, fair, good, excellent). Position-biased implicit feedback (clicks, add-to-cart) is not used directly as ground truth; instead, labels are obtained independently of click history to avoid position bias.
structured_choices.label_definition: Crowdsourced relevance judgments: annotators rate each query-product pair on a 5-point scale. A pair is considered 'relevant' (positive) if rated 'good' or 'excellent'; otherwise 'not relevant' (negative). Judgment is made blind to click frequency and ranking position in the production system. A minimum of 3 annotations per pair with majority vote used for the final label; pairs with disagreement > 1 point are adjudicated by senior annotators or excluded from test set.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal-stratified split on the judged label set only (since most raw data is not labeled). Of 5,000 labeled pairs: train on pairs from months 1–3 (3,000 pairs), validation on month 4 (1,000 pairs), and test on months 5–6 (1,000 pairs). Within each fold, stratify by query frequency tier (head, mid-tail, long-tail) to ensure each split contains representative coverage. Query logs (unlabeled) from months 1–4 are used for training ranking features and computing click feedback aggregates; month 5–6 logs are held out to compute test-time features without information leakage. This temporal split prevents leakage of recent click signals into training and ensures the model is evaluated on truly held-out temporal data. Stratification by query tier ensures long-tail queries are represented in all folds.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: BM25 statistics (IDF, term frequencies) are computed on the product catalog as of month 1 (frozen snapshot); they are not updated during the experiment to avoid catalog churn confounding. Query-product semantic embeddings (via a pre-trained dense retriever, e.g., DPR or ColBERT) are computed offline using the same frozen catalog; embedding model is not fine-tuned in this experiment, ensuring a fair comparison (both models use the same retrieval index). Click feedback aggregates (e.g., click-through rate, add-to-cart rate per query-product pair) are computed separately for train (months 1–3), validation (month 4), and test (months 5–6) windows to avoid look-ahead bias. No scaling or normalization of ranking features is fit on training data and applied universally; instead, learning-to-rank models (XGBoost, LambdaMART) are trained with their native feature handling. If feature scaling is used, it is fit independently within each fold to prevent data leakage.
structured_choices.features: For the learning-to-rank model: (1) BM25 score (title + description); (2) semantic similarity (cosine similarity between query embedding and product embedding); (3) click-through rate (CTR) of the query-product pair in the training window; (4) add-to-cart rate (ATC) in the training window; (5) query length and product catalog recency (days since product was added/updated); (6) query frequency tier (binary: head vs. tail); (7) product popularity (global CTR and ATC). All click-based features are recomputed per fold to avoid temporal leakage. Baseline (BM25) uses only BM25 score. Both models are evaluated on the same held-out test set, ensuring fair comparison. Features are not derived from test-set click data.
structured_choices.model: Learning-to-rank model: LambdaMART (via LightGBM ranking mode) or XGBoost with ranking loss (pairwise or listwise), trained on relevance labels with ranking features as input. Hyperparameters (tree depth, learning rate, number of rounds) are tuned on the validation set via grid search or Bayesian optimization. The model outputs a relevance score for each query-product pair; ranking is performed by sorting by this score. Alternative configurations (e.g., linear learner with hand-tuned feature weights, neural ranker) are not explored in the primary design, but the design is not restricted to one model class.
structured_choices.baseline: BM25-only retrieval: ranking is determined solely by BM25 score (no learned reranking). The BM25 baseline is not a hyperparameter-free strawman; instead, BM25 parameters (k1, b) are tuned on the validation set using the same primary metric (NDCG@10) via grid search over reasonable ranges (k1 ∈ [1.2, 2.5], b ∈ [0.5, 0.8]). This ensures the baseline receives equivalent tuning effort and is as strong as reasonably possible. Both baseline and LTR model are evaluated on the same test set under identical conditions. This comparison isolates the value of learning-to-rank features (embeddings + click feedback) versus retrieval-only ranking.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at rank 10 (NDCG@10), computed using the manually judged relevance labels on the test set. NDCG@10 is chosen because: (1) users typically examine the first 10 results before refining a query, aligning with the business goal of showing relevant products 'earlier'; (2) it accounts for graded relevance (not just binary), capturing partial relevance; (3) it down-weights lower-ranked items (discounting), which prioritizes top-rank accuracy; (4) normalization makes it comparable across queries of different label distributions. NDCG@10 directly operationalizes the business objective of improved relevance in the search results. Test NDCG@10 is computed separately for head, mid-tail, and long-tail queries to validate the hypothesis that gains are 'especially for long-tail queries'; we expect larger ΔNDCG in the long-tail bucket.
secondary_metrics: Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) — captures user satisfaction if a single highly relevant item is sought
Precision@10 and Recall@10 — measure coverage of relevant items in top-10
NDCG@5 — sensitivity test to ensure gains are not limited to lower ranks
Per-query-tier breakdown (head/mid/tail NDCG@10) — to validate the long-tail hypothesis specifically
Offline NDCG@10 on synonym-heavy queries (subset of test set) — to evaluate the semantic embedding component
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (month 4, 1,000 labeled pairs) is used for hyperparameter tuning. Grid search is performed over LambdaMART hyperparameters (num_leaves ∈ [31, 127], learning_rate ∈ [0.01, 0.1], num_rounds ∈ [100, 500]) and BM25 parameters, each configuration evaluated on the validation set using NDCG@10. The configuration with the highest validation NDCG@10 is selected for final evaluation on the test set. Validation performance is monitored but is not used as the reported result; test set performance is the sole basis for the hypothesis verdict.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 5–6, 1,000 labeled pairs) is locked and not touched until after model selection is complete. Once the final model and baseline are trained on the training set and tuned on the validation set, test set NDCG@10 (and secondary metrics) are computed exactly once. No further tuning or model adjustments are made after test-set evaluation. All reported results come from this single test-set evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Position bias and freshness effects: The hypothesis is tested using manually judged labels, not implicit feedback, so position bias in click data does not affect the primary metric. Catalog churn: Product embeddings and BM25 IDF are computed from a frozen catalog snapshot (month 1), ensuring both models operate on the same product representation. Popularity priors: Click-based features (CTR, ATC) are included in the LTR model but not the baseline, so any improvements from popularity signals are legitimately part of the 'learning-to-rank' contribution (not a confound); however, we separately evaluate whether gains persist on low-popularity items by reporting NDCG@10 for products in the bottom quartile of catalog popularity. Query seasonality: The temporal split (train/val/test) ensures the model is evaluated on future data, mitigating the risk of seasonal artifacts. Head-query bias: Stratification by query tier ensures the test set contains sufficient long-tail queries; the per-tier breakdown (secondary metric) directly tests whether gains are larger in the long-tail, as hypothesized. If the LTR model improves head-query ranking much more than long-tail, the hypothesis would be partially unsupported.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Learning-to-Rank vs. BM25 in E-commerce Search

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses query logs, product catalog text, and interaction events (clicks, add-to-cart) from a 6-month production window. To avoid position bias—a critical confound in search evaluation—we do not use click-through rates directly as relevance labels. Instead, we commission crowdsourced relevance judgments on a stratified sample of 5,000 query-product pairs. These pairs are drawn proportionally from three frequency tiers (head, mid-tail, long-tail queries) to ensure the test set is representative of the full query distribution. Within the long-tail tier, we oversample ambiguous and synonym-heavy queries to create a subset for targeted analysis. Each pair is annotated by at least three independent raters on a 5-point relevance scale (irrelevant to excellent). Pairs with high inter-rater disagreement (>1 point) are adjudicated by senior annotators or excluded. This yields a reliable ground truth divorced from ranking position and click bias.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

The 5,000 labeled pairs are divided temporally and stratified: training (months 1–3, 3,000 pairs), validation (month 4, 1,000 pairs), and test (months 5–6, 1,000 pairs). Each fold is internally stratified by query tier. This temporal split prevents leakage of recent click signals and catalog changes into training. The held-out query logs from months 5–6 are used only to compute test-time features (such as click aggregates), ensuring no information from the test period influences model training. Stratification guarantees that long-tail queries—the focus of the hypothesis—are represented in all splits.

### 3. Features and Preprocessing

The learning-to-rank model uses seven features: BM25 score, semantic similarity (from pre-trained embeddings), query-product click-through rate, add-to-cart rate, query length, product recency, and product popularity. BM25 parameters (k1, b) and semantic embeddings are computed from a frozen catalog snapshot (month 1) to prevent catalog churn from confounding comparisons. Click-based features are computed separately for each temporal fold, using only in-window interactions, to avoid look-ahead bias. The baseline receives the same treatment: it uses only the BM25 score, but BM25 parameters are tuned on the validation set to ensure the baseline is as strong as possible. This fair tuning of the baseline isolates the contribution of additional features (embeddings, click feedback) to the learning-to-rank model.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The learning-to-rank model is LambdaMART (LightGBM ranking) or XGBoost with pairwise ranking loss, trained on the training set relevance labels. Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set via grid search, optimizing NDCG@10. The baseline is BM25-only retrieval; its parameters are similarly tuned on the validation set, ensuring equivalent effort and preventing the baseline from being a straw man. Both are evaluated on the identical held-out test set.

### 5. Evaluation and Validation

The primary metric is NDCG@10, computed using manually judged labels on the test set. NDCG@10 is chosen because it reflects the business goal (better relevance in top results), accounts for graded relevance, and down-weights lower ranks. Secondary metrics include MRR, P@10, R@10, and per-tier NDCG@10 breakdowns. To validate the hypothesis specifically, we report NDCG@10 separately for head, mid-tail, and long-tail queries; if the model outperforms the baseline more strongly in long-tail, the hypothesis is supported. Model selection uses only the validation set (NDCG@10); the test set is not touched until after hyperparameter tuning is complete, ensuring an unbiased final evaluation.

### 6. Confound Controls

Position bias is eliminated by using manual labels instead of clicks. Catalog churn is controlled by freezing the product index and embeddings. Popularity priors are isolated: click features in the LTR model are a legitimate component of learning-to-rank, but secondary analysis on low-popularity items tests whether improvements generalize beyond popular products. Query seasonality is mitigated by the temporal split. The design thus isolates the causal effect of semantic embeddings and learned ranking on relevance, relative to retrieval-only ranking.
